sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.content: Tobacco use (cigarettes, cigars, pipes, smokeless tobacco) - Strongly associated with oral cavity, oropharynx (HPV-negative), hypopharynx, larynx, and nasopharynx cancers. Risk increases with duration and intensity (pack-years). Smoking decreases efficacy of radiotherapy and adversely affects survival [Discussion MS-5, MS-21]. Strength: Established causal relationship; RR 5-25 for current smokers vs. never-smokers. Alcohol consumption - Synergistic with tobacco; particularly for oral cavity, oropharynx, hypopharynx, and larynx cancers. Heavy drinking (≥3 drinks/day) significantly increases risk [Discussion MS-5]. Strength: Established causal; RR 2-5 for heavy drinkers vs. nondrinkers, synergistic with tobacco. Human papillomavirus (HPV) infection, especially HPV16 - Primary cause of oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (tonsil and base of tongue). HPV16 accounts for ~90% of HPV-positive cases. Oral HPV infection increases risk of oropharyngeal cancer [Discussion MS-3]. Strength: Causal; attributable fraction ~90% in US oropharyngeal cancers; OR ~15-50 for HPV16 seropositivity. Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection - Key etiologic factor for nasopharyngeal carcinoma, particularly in endemic regions. EBV DNA detectable in tumor tissue and plasma; high viral load associated with worse prognosis [Discussion MS-41]. Strength: Causal; EBV positivity in >95% of nonkeratinizing nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Occupational exposures (wood dust, leather, nickel, formaldehyde) - Specifically for sinonasal cancers. Wood dust exposure is strongly linked to adenocarcinoma of the ethmoid sinus. Nickel refining, leather dust, and formaldehyde are also associated [Discussion MS-52]. Strength: Established; wood dust RR ~5-50 for ethmoid adenocarcinoma. Genetic predisposition - Family history of head and neck cancer increases risk. Patients with Fanconi anemia, dyskeratosis congenita, and Li-Fraumeni syndrome have elevated risk. Genome-wide association studies have identified risk loci at 4q21, 4q25, 12q24, and other regions [OR-1]. Strength: Moderate to high in specific syndromes; modest for common variants. Male sex - Incidence rates 2-4 times higher in men across most subsites. For oropharyngeal cancer, the male-to-female ratio is 4-5:1 for HPV-positive cases [Discussion MS-3]. Strength: Established; male sex is an independent risk factor. Immunosuppression (HIV/AIDS, organ transplantation) - Increased risk of HPV-related head and neck cancers. HIV-positive individuals have higher rates of oral and oropharyngeal cancers [Discussion MS-4]. Strength: Well-established; RR 2-6 in HIV/AIDS. Betel quid and areca nut chewing - Common in South and Southeast Asia; associated with oral cavity and oropharyngeal cancers. Synergistic with tobacco and alcohol [Discussion MS-5]. Strength: Causal; RR 5-20 for betel quid chewers. Poor oral hygiene and chronic irritation - Chronic dental trauma, poor dental status, and chronic oral infections may contribute to oral cavity cancer risk [Discussion MS-31]. Strength: Weak to moderate; evidence limited.

cards.factor: Tobacco use (cigarettes, cigars, pipes, smokeless tobacco)
cards.detail: Strongly associated with oral cavity, oropharynx (HPV-negative), hypopharynx, larynx, and nasopharynx cancers. Risk increases with duration and intensity (pack-years). Smoking decreases efficacy of radiotherapy and adversely affects survival [Discussion MS-5, MS-21].
Synergistic with tobacco; particularly for oral cavity, oropharynx, hypopharynx, and larynx cancers. Heavy drinking (≥3 drinks/day) significantly increases risk [Discussion MS-5].

Primary cause of oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (tonsil and base of tongue). HPV16 accounts for ~90% of HPV-positive cases. Oral HPV infection increases risk of oropharyngeal cancer [Discussion MS-3].
Key etiologic factor for nasopharyngeal carcinoma, particularly in endemic regions. EBV DNA detectable in tumor tissue and plasma; high viral load associated with worse prognosis [Discussion MS-41].

Incidence rates 2-4 times higher in men across most subsites. For oropharyngeal cancer, the male-to-female ratio is 4-5:1 for HPV-positive cases [Discussion MS-3].
Increased risk of HPV-related head and neck cancers. HIV-positive individuals have higher rates of oral and oropharyngeal cancers [Discussion MS-4].
